0.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for an initial consult regarding your breast cancer treatment.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. It is now in an advanced stage (Stage IV) and has spread to other parts of your body, including your lungs, the lining of your abdomen (belly area), your liver, and your ovaries.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
A CT scan showed that the cancer has spread widely. You have a mass in your armpit area that needs to be checked. A brain MRI and a bone scan were ordered to complete your tests.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
If the biopsy shows that the cancer is hormone receptor positive and HER2 negative, you may be recommended to take a medication called ibrance and another medication.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You are referred to Integrative Medicine for natural therapies. You have an appointment with your doctor on Thursday to biopsy the mass in your armpit. After all the tests are completed, we will meet again to decide on a treatment plan. Your advance care status is noted as full code.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a medical oncology consult regarding your newly diagnosed breast cancer.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. It is in the early stage (Stage IIA) and has not spread to other parts of your body.
The goal of your treatment is to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
A PET scan and genetic testing are pending.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
We discussed the use of chemotherapy to help reduce the risk of the cancer coming back. We also talked about surgery and radiation to lower the risk of the cancer returning in the same area.
**What is the plan going forward?**
Your advance care status is noted as full code.
We understand that this can be a difficult time, and we are here to support you.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

7.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a consultation to discuss treatment options and to establish care after receiving an incomplete course of treatment before surgery and undergoing surgery to remove the tumor and check the lymph nodes.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. The cancer is in an advanced stage and has not spread to other parts of your body. The goal of treatment is to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
No new blood tests were done during this visit.
An imaging test called a PET/CT scan was done previously and showed no signs of the cancer spreading.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You are currently taking pain medication called oxyCODONE.
No changes were made to your current medications.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You will start a new treatment plan that includes chemotherapy to prevent the cancer from coming back, followed by another type of treatment called T-DM1.
You will also need radiation after completing the chemotherapy.
You will need to have a small device called a port placed to help with the chemotherapy.
You will need an imaging test called an echocardiogram before starting the chemotherapy.
You are referred to social work for support.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

11.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a follow-up visit regarding your breast cancer treatment.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. It has spread to other parts of your body, including your brain, lungs, and bones.
The treatment is aimed at making you feel better and controlling the cancer, not curing it.
Recent imaging shows that the cancer in your bones and lungs is stable, but there are new small areas of cancer in your brain.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
No new blood tests were done.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You will continue taking herceptin, letrozole, and another medication. You will also continue taking pain medicine as needed.
You will not be getting chemotherapy for now because you had trouble with it before.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You will have more imaging tests every 4 months to check on the cancer.
You are referred to a radiation oncologist for possible more radiation treatment to your brain.
You will see your doctor again in 6 weeks.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

END OF IMPRESSION    Assessment and Recommendations:  64 y.o. postmenopausal patient with a clinical stage II-III *****-/*****+ IDC of the right breast who should proceed with neoadjuvant chemotherapy.    We reviewed the available imaging and pathology reports.  We discussed her original DCIS diagnosis from 2007 as well as a second biopsy in 2013 that was concerning for microinvasive disease; she did not undergo conventional therapy at the time of her original diagnoses.  I 
 explained to her that she now had clear evidence of invasive breast cancer, but noted that her PET/CT demonstrated no evidence of metastases and therefore she can now be treated with curative intent.  I discussed with ***** and her husband in great detail the natural history and treatment of early stage *****-/*****+ IDC.  I explained that were she to forego the recommended therapy, I am very concerned that she would develop metastatic disease in the short term, even in the context of her 10 year history of DCIS.    We discussed the multimodality approach to breast cancer treatment. The patient understands that her treatment recommendations will include chemotherapy, biological therapies (trastuzumab and pertuzumab), surgery, and radiation. We discussed the advantages of offering chemotherapy in the neoadjuvant setting in order to increase the chances of breast conserving surgery, while concurrently allowing us to assess the response to treatment.  I explained that in the context of her high-risk node-negative, ***** positive disease we would typically recommend that she receive either 12 cycles of weekly paclitaxel plus trastuzumab/pertuzumab (THP) followed by 4 cycles of doxorubicin plus cyclophosphamide (AC) every 2 weeks or 6 cycles of every 3 week docetaxel, carboplatin, trastuzumab, and pertuzumab (TCHP).  I also explained that either regimen would be followed by every 3 week trastuzumab for a total of one year of biological therapy.     We reviewed the potential side effects of both regimens.  Given her extensive disease even in the absence of nodal involvement, I recommend that she proceed with an anthracycline-containing regimen, unless she is found to have substantial cardiac dysfunction on TTE.  I specifically discussed with ***** the typical and less common side-effects associated with THP/AC, such as: fatigue, nausea, myelosuppression, nail changes, alopecia, diarrhea, and constipation. I stated that 
 with regards to trastuzumab, she is at risk of a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, which can result in asymptomatic or symptomatic heart failure with dyspnea and chest pain.  I stated that pertuzumab is associated with diarrhea and rash.  We additionally discussed the rare adverse events associated with THP/AC, both secondary leukemia and cardiomyopathy.      After an extensive discussion, the patient completely understands the rationale for neoadjuvant treatment and will spend the weekend considering where she wants to proceed with her therapy.  I explained to her that I would recommend she start her treatment within 2 to 3 weeks.  She would need a TTE, Mediport placement, and if treated here at *****, a chemotherapy teaching session.    The patient will contact me after the weekend and let me know whether she plans to proceed with treatment here at ***** or elsewhere, closer to home.    I spent a total of 120 minutes face-to-face with the patient and 115 minutes of that time was spent counseling regarding the diagnosis, the treatment plan, the prognosis, medication risks, lifestyle modification, the risks and benefits of surgery, symptoms and therapeutic options.

Reason_for_Visit.summary: 64 y.o. postmenopausal patient with a recent diagnosis of early stage breast cancer, specifically [REDACTED]-/[REDACTED]+ IDC of the right breast, presenting in consultation to discuss treatment options and to establish care.

Clinical_Findings.findings: Right breast with a 6 x 11 cm mass in the outer quadrants. No palpable masses in the left breast. No lymphadenopathy noted on physical exam. Imaging findings include a large enhancing right breast mass (9.0 x 3.8 cm, SUV 7.8) on PET/CT, with two mildly hypermetabolic right axillary lymph nodes (0.6 cm and 0.7 cm with SUV max 1.8) and an enhancing hypermetabolic 7 mm lymph node in the subcutaneous fat of the right chest wall (SUV 2.9). MRI of the breast shows marked NME with clustered ring internal enhancement extending throughout the entire right breast with extension to the right nipple and no pectoralis invasion. There is an enlarged right internal mammary chain node (0.6 cm). Nonspecific hypodensity in hepatic segment  3 (0.6 cm) and nonspecific splenic hypodensity (0.8 cm) are noted, both likely benign. FDG uptake at the level of the coccyx (SUV 5.0) is favored to be inflammatory or posttraumatic. Pathology results show invasive ductal carcinoma (0.35 cm) and intermediate to high grade DCIS. The tumor is ER- (0%), PR- (0%), HER2+ (IHC 3; FISH ratio 8.9), and has a Ki-67 proliferation index of ~30%.

Medication_Plan.medication_plan: The patient will start neoadjuvant chemotherapy with either 12 cycles of weekly paclitaxel plus trastuzumab/pertuzumab (THP) followed by 4 cycles of doxorubicin plus cyclophosphamide (AC) every 2 weeks or 6 cycles of every 3 week docetaxel, carboplatin, trastuzumab, and pertuzumab (TCHP). This will be followed by every 3 week trastuzumab for a total of one year of biological therapy.

Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a consultation to discuss treatment options for your early stage breast cancer.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. It is at an early stage, but it has spread to some lymph nodes. The goal of treatment is to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
Your blood tests are mostly normal, with a creatinine level of 0.74. You will need an echocardiogram (TTE) before starting chemotherapy.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You will start a treatment plan that includes chemotherapy and biological therapies. The chemotherapy will be given before surgery to shrink the cancer.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You will have a port placed to make it easier to give the chemotherapy. You will decide after the weekend whether you want to start treatment here or closer to home.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

43.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a follow-up visit regarding your breast cancer treatment.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. There is also some cancer in the milk-producing glands (DCIS). The cancer is in your left breast and has spread to some lymph nodes.
The purpose of this visit was to discuss radiation treatment and a clinical trial.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
Your recent imaging tests for neck and back pain were negative. A small nodule in your lung that was found earlier is stable.
A follow-up CT scan of your chest is planned for one year from now.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You are taking some pain medication as needed and a stool softener.
You will start a medication called an aromatase inhibitor after you finish radiation treatment. This medication helps prevent the cancer from coming back.
If there is a delay in your surgery to remove your ovaries, you may need a medication called Zoladex.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You will be randomized to either 3 or 5 weeks of radiation treatment. The tentative start date is December 16, 2018.
You are referred to radiation oncology for further treatment planning.
You are also referred to physical therapy to help with stiffness in your shoulders and arms.
You will have a follow-up visit with us on January 5, 2019.
You will also follow up with nutrition on November 30, 2018, to help with your weight gain.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

You will be randomized to either 3 or 5 weeks of radiation treatment. The tentative start date is December 16, 2018.
You are referred to radiation oncology for further treatment planning.
You are also referred to physical therapy to help with stiffness in your shoulders and arms.
You will have a follow-up visit with us on January 5, 2019.
You will also follow up with nutrition on November 30, 2018, to help with your weight gain.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team
